questions.stem: Read the passage and answer. "The harvest failed because the rains came too late. By the time the seeds were planted, the soil had already dried out." Why did the harvest fail?
options: Because the market prices fell
Because the seeds were of poor quality
Because there were too many farmers
Because the rains came too late
questions.explanation: A literal comprehension question is answered from what the text states directly. The passage says plainly that the harvest failed because the rains came too late, leaving the soil dry by planting time. The other options are not mentioned in the passage.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: The main aim of summarising a passage is to
options: express the main ideas briefly in your own words
make the passage longer and more detailed
copy the passage word for word
add your personal opinion to the passage
questions.explanation: A summary restates the essential ideas of a text in a much shorter form and in the writer's own words. It must not copy the original, add new opinions, or lengthen the text. The goal is brevity with faithfulness to the main points.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Which of the following should be left out of a good summary?
options: Minor examples and repetitions
The central idea of the passage
The main supporting points
The overall conclusion
questions.explanation: A summary keeps the central idea, the main supporting points, and the conclusion, but it removes minor details, illustrations, and anything repeated. Cutting the minor examples and repetitions is what makes a summary short while preserving the substance.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "Although the bridge was old, engineers declared it safe after careful inspection." What can be correctly inferred?
options: The bridge was newly built.
The engineers refused to inspect the bridge.
The bridge was inspected before being declared safe.
The bridge was unsafe and was closed.
questions.explanation: An inference must follow logically from the words on the page. The passage states that the engineers declared the bridge safe after careful inspection, so it is sound to infer that an inspection took place before the verdict. The other options contradict the passage.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "Plastic waste chokes our rivers, harms fish, and eventually returns to us through the food we eat." Which sentence best summarises it?
options: Rivers are the only place where plastic is found.
People should eat more fish from rivers.
Plastic waste damages rivers and wildlife and can affect human health.
Fish enjoy living among plastic waste.
questions.explanation: A good one sentence summary captures the central idea and main points without distortion. The passage links plastic waste to harm to rivers, to fish, and finally to people, and the second option gathers all three points faithfully. The others misread or narrow the passage.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "She studied late into the night, determined not to repeat last year's failure." What does the passage suggest about her?
options: She had already passed every exam.
She had never sat an exam before.
She disliked studying of any kind.
She had failed before and wanted to do better.
questions.explanation: The phrase "not to repeat last year's failure" implies that she failed in the past and is now working hard to avoid the same result. This inference rests directly on the wording. The other options conflict with that wording.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: When writing a summary, the writer should normally use
options: direct speech copied from the passage
the third person and a neutral, reporting tone
exclamations and rhetorical questions
the first person and personal feelings
questions.explanation: A summary reports the content of a passage objectively, so it normally uses the third person and a neutral tone rather than personal feelings or dramatic devices. Copying direct speech works against the aim of compressing the text in one's own words.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "The new policy raised fuel prices. As a result, the cost of transport rose, and market traders increased the prices of goods." According to the passage, what was the first effect of the policy?
options: Market traders increased the prices of goods.
The policy was cancelled.
Fuel prices rose.
The cost of transport rose.
questions.explanation: Tracing a chain of cause and effect requires reading the order carefully. The passage shows the policy first raised fuel prices, which then raised transport costs, which then raised goods prices. So the first effect of the policy was the rise in fuel prices.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "The lecture was so dull that several students struggled to stay awake." What is the meaning of the word "dull" as used here?
options: short
bright
loud
boring
questions.explanation: Word meaning in context is judged by the surrounding sense. Because the lecture made students struggle to stay awake, "dull" must mean boring or uninteresting. "Bright," "loud," and "short" do not fit the idea of nearly falling asleep.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: A good summary should be
options: much shorter than the original yet faithful to its meaning
a list of every detail in the passage
longer than the original passage
the same length as the original
questions.explanation: Summarising compresses a passage to a fraction of its length while keeping its essential meaning. A summary that matches or exceeds the original length, or that lists every detail, defeats the purpose of summarising.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "Despite heavy rain, the farmers continued to work, knowing the planting season was short." Why did the farmers keep working in the rain?
options: They were told to stop working.
They enjoyed working in wet weather.
The planting season was short.
They had nothing else to do.
questions.explanation: The reason is stated directly in the passage: they kept working because the planting season was short, so the rain could not be allowed to stop them. The other options are not supported by the text.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "The manager praised the team for finishing the project ahead of schedule and within budget." Which two reasons earned the team praise?
options: Finishing early and staying within budget
Taking long breaks and arriving late
Working slowly and overspending
Hiring new staff and buying equipment
questions.explanation: The passage names exactly two grounds for praise: finishing ahead of schedule and staying within budget. The correct option lists both. The others describe behaviour the passage does not mention or that contradicts the praise.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "By dawn the floodwater had receded, leaving behind a thick layer of mud on every street." What does "receded" mean here?
options: moved back or fallen
disappeared completely
risen higher
frozen solid
questions.explanation: Context fixes the meaning of "receded." The floodwater left mud behind by dawn, which shows the water had drawn back and fallen rather than risen. "Disappeared completely" is too strong, since mud remained, and "risen" and "frozen" contradict the scene.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the tone of this sentence? "At last, after years of struggle, she held her degree and beamed with joy."
options: fearful
indifferent
joyful
angry
questions.explanation: Tone is the writer's attitude conveyed through word choice. Words like "at last," "beamed," and "joy" express happiness and relief, so the tone is joyful. Nothing in the sentence suggests anger, fear, or indifference.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: In summary writing, changing the writer's words into your own words is called
options: copying
translating
paraphrasing
quoting
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing means restating someone else's ideas in your own words while keeping the meaning. Quoting reproduces the exact words, copying repeats them without acknowledgement, and translating moves text into another language. Summarising relies on paraphrasing.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "The shop sells fruits, vegetables, and bread, but it does not sell meat or fish." Which item can you NOT buy at the shop?
options: fruits
fish
bread
vegetables
questions.explanation: The passage lists what the shop sells and then states what it does not sell. Fish is named among the items the shop does not sell, so it cannot be bought there. Bread, vegetables, and fruits are all listed as available.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "The scientist repeated the experiment three times to be sure the result was not due to chance." Why did the scientist repeat the experiment?
options: To waste time
Because the first result was lost
To confirm the result was reliable and not accidental
To use up the chemicals
questions.explanation: The passage gives the purpose directly: the repetition was meant to be sure the result was not due to chance, that is, to confirm it was reliable. The other options are not stated and do not match the stated purpose.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "Few visitors come to the village now, for the only road leading to it has fallen into ruin." What is the main reason few visitors come?
options: The villagers dislike visitors.
The village is too crowded.
The only road to the village is in ruins.
There is nothing to see in the village.
questions.explanation: The word "for" introduces the reason: few visitors come because the only road has fallen into ruin and is hard to use. The passage gives no support for crowding, hostility, or a lack of sights as the reason.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "The athlete trained daily, ate carefully, and slept well; unsurprisingly, she won the race." The word "unsurprisingly" suggests that the writer regards her victory as
options: a matter of pure luck
an unfair result
a shocking accident
an expected outcome of her preparation
questions.explanation: The word "unsurprisingly" signals that the result was to be expected given what came before. Because she trained, ate, and slept well, the writer presents her win as the natural outcome of careful preparation, not an accident or luck.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: When you summarise several long sentences into one, the best approach is to
options: pick the longest sentence and copy it
remove every verb to save space
identify the shared main idea and state it concisely
join all the sentences with the word and
questions.explanation: Effective summarising looks past the surface wording to the shared main idea and expresses it concisely in new words. Copying one sentence, stringing them together with "and," or stripping out verbs would either lose meaning or fail to compress the text properly.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "Mobile phones connect people across great distances, yet some users complain that they reduce face to face conversation." Which statement best reflects the balanced view in the passage?
options: Mobile phones have only benefits.
Mobile phones have only drawbacks.
Mobile phones are no longer used by anyone.
Mobile phones bring people together but may reduce direct conversation.
questions.explanation: The passage presents both a benefit and a complaint, joined by "yet." A faithful summary must keep both sides: phones connect people over distance but may cut down on direct conversation. Options claiming only benefits or only drawbacks ignore half the passage.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "The teacher explained the rule twice, gave three examples, and still some pupils looked puzzled." What does the passage imply?
options: Some pupils did not understand the rule despite the teacher's efforts.
The rule was easy for everyone.
The teacher refused to explain the rule.
There were no examples given.
questions.explanation: The word "still" sets the teacher's repeated effort against the pupils' continued confusion, implying that some pupils did not grasp the rule even after two explanations and three examples. The other options contradict details in the passage.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "Tourism brings income to the town, but the crowds also strain its water supply and roads." A complete summary of this passage must mention
options: both the income and the strain on resources
only the income from tourism
neither the income nor the strain
only the strain on water and roads
questions.explanation: The passage makes a two sided point joined by "but." A complete summary must keep both sides: tourism brings income and at the same time strains the town's water and roads. Leaving out either side would misrepresent the passage.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise

questions.stem: Read the passage. "He spoke softly, choosing each word with care, so as not to alarm the frightened child." Why did he speak softly and carefully?
options: Because the room was very noisy
To frighten the child further
Because he was angry with the child
To avoid alarming the frightened child
questions.explanation: The phrase "so as not to alarm the frightened child" states his purpose directly. He spoke softly and chose his words carefully in order to avoid alarming the child. The remaining options run counter to that stated purpose.
questions.source: in the style of a standard reading comprehension exercise
